card.title: Why You Need an Emergency Fund
card.teaser: A broken car, a surprise bill, a lost job — what stands between you and a 22% APR credit card?
card.coverImagePrompt: A glass jar on a wooden table half-filled with neatly stacked banknotes and coins, soft window light, warm morning tones, shallow focus

lessons.title: The $400 question
lessons.body: The U.S. Federal Reserve regularly asks Americans a simple question: could you cover a $400 emergency expense without borrowing? For years, more than a third said no. That's not a story about poverty — it's a story about living with zero margin. And margin is the entire point of an emergency fund.

visual.imagePrompt: A cracked smartphone screen next to a coffee cup, with a printed repair quote showing $400

lessons.title: What it actually is
lessons.body: An emergency fund is a pile of cash you never spend on anything except genuine emergencies: job loss, surprise medical bills, a car you can't commute without. It doesn't earn glamorous returns — and that's the feature, not the bug. Its job is to be boring, liquid, and there.

keyTerms.definition: Easy to convert to cash without losing value
The return you give up by holding cash instead of investing

visual.imagePrompt: A savings account statement with a highlighted balance, clean minimalist design

lessons.title: Three to six months — and why
lessons.body: The standard rule is three to six months of essential expenses. Not your full lifestyle — just rent, food, insurance, utilities, minimum debt payments. Three months if you have a stable salary and a partner with income. Six or more if you're self-employed, have dependents, or work in a cyclical industry. The fund buys you time to make decisions instead of panic-taking the first job offer.

visual.imagePrompt: A clean infographic showing three, six, and nine month 'buckets' of essential expenses

lessons.title: The math of borrowing instead
lessons.body: Say an AC unit dies and costs $3,000. Put it on a credit card at 22% APR and pay $100/month, and you'll end up paying back roughly $4,100 over three and a half years. With an emergency fund, the same repair costs $3,000. Flat. The fund isn't really about the emergency — it's about not paying a private interest tax on every bad day of your life.
lessons.funFact: A 2023 Bankrate survey found that only 44% of Americans could cover a $1,000 surprise expense from savings alone.

visual.imagePrompt: A line chart showing two curves — flat cash vs rising credit card balance over 3 years

lessons.title: Start ugly, start small
lessons.body: The first $1,000 matters most. It covers the vast majority of real-world emergencies and breaks the borrow-to-survive cycle. Put it in a high-yield savings account in a bank separate from your checking — friction is a feature. Then build toward three months. The goal isn't perfection. It's never letting a bad week become a bad year.

quiz.question: What is the recommended size of a typical emergency fund?
options: Three to six months of essential expenses
quiz.explanation: Three to six months of essential (not total) expenses is the standard target.

quiz.question: Why should an emergency fund be held in a liquid account?
options: To earn the highest possible return
So it can be accessed quickly without penalty
Because it grows faster that way
quiz.explanation: Emergencies don't wait — you need to access the money without selling investments or paying withdrawal penalties.

quiz.question: What is the main financial cost of not having an emergency fund?
options: Lower credit score from unused cards
Paying high interest on emergency borrowing
Missing out on stock market returns
quiz.explanation: Without cash on hand, emergencies get charged to high-interest debt — often at 20%+ APR.

sources.title: Report on the Economic Well-Being of U.S. Households
